overall_summary: Tesla broadly supports the Commission's reform principles and offers refinements drawn from its Gigafactory Texas experience, urging clearer and more predictable large-load interconnection. It pushes for expedited reviews of behind-the-meter storage that offsets generation loss, standardized technology-neutral minimum interconnection standards, and recognition of distributed energy resources and virtual power plants as tools to ease transmission constraints.

quotes.text: Tesla is generally supportive of concepts that limit duplicative load interconnection requests; however, we believe that requirements should be instituted for large loads presumed to be data centers or crypto mining centers.
quotes.concern: Supports study deposits and readiness requirements, targeted at data center and crypto large loads

quotes.text: a system should be established to provide credit back for study deposits, but perhaps through transmission credits.
quotes.concern: Wants study deposits refundable via transmission credits

quotes.text: Tesla strongly endorses the fifth principle, which notes that hybrid facilities should be studied based on the amount of injection and/or withdrawal rights requested to incentivize colocation and efficiency buildout.
quotes.concern: Strongly endorses studying hybrid facilities by injection/withdrawal rights to incentivize colocation

quotes.text: We suggest that any additional load or battery energy storage systems (BESS) installed behind-the-meter (BTM) at large load facilities count towards the point of interconnection (POI) limit.
quotes.concern: Behind-the-meter load and storage should count toward POI limit

quotes.text: Tesla believes that capacity additions behind the same POI that offset generation “loss” to serve load should be permitted with expedited reviews, assuming the system maintains consistent or similar POI import/export limitations.
quotes.concern: Wants expedited review for storage offsetting generation loss at same POI

quotes.text: Tesla recommends establishing minimum interconnection standards for large loads, including ramp rate, load stability, and voltage ride-through rate as standardized with the process.
quotes.concern: Recommends standardized minimum interconnection standards for large loads

quotes.text: These should be applied at the POI and with a technology-neutral lens. Similar performance expectations are being applied or developed in regions (e.g. SPP and ERCOT) and could serve as a foundation for national best practices.
quotes.concern: Standards should be technology-neutral and build on SPP and ERCOT practice

quotes.text: if a load meets the minimum interconnection standards, additional information on its makeup, which may change over time, should not be required—especially at early stages in the load’s development when it may not be available.
quotes.concern: Once standards are met, no further load-makeup disclosure should be required

quotes.text: This experience highlights a broader need to establish greater clarity, efficiency, and predictability in the interconnection process as large load growth driven by manufacturing facilities and data centers continues at a rapid pace.
quotes.concern: Urges clarity, efficiency, and predictability in the interconnection process

quotes.text: Tesla urges the recognition of DERs and VPPs as meaningful grid service tools that can support faster interconnection of large loads to the transmission system.
quotes.concern: Urges recognition of DERs and VPPs as tools for faster large-load interconnection

quotes.text: VPPs should be considered for their potential to alleviate transmission network constraints and capacity needs associated with the interconnection of large loads.
quotes.concern: VPPs should be counted as capacity that relieves transmission constraints

quotes.text: restrictive interpretations of Order No. 2222 continue to limit the participation of these resources. Currently, no interpretation of the Order practically enables the full value of an inverter-based DER.

FERC must address challenges in 2222 implementations including: 1) metering and telemetry barriers, 2) device size thresholds, 3) state-level barriers, and particularly 4) the lack of pathways for DERs receiving retail rate credits.
quotes.concern: Lists specific Order 2222 barriers FERC must fix

bins.name: Readiness rules for speculative loads
bins.description: Tesla supports mechanisms that limit duplicative load interconnection requests, and argues readiness and study-deposit requirements should target large loads presumed to be data centers or crypto mining centers.

bins.name: Refundable study deposits
bins.description: Tesla backs study deposits and withdrawal penalties for large loads but wants the deposits credited back, possibly through transmission credits, since prior load requests were not subject to such mechanisms.

bins.name: Deposit credit through transmission
bins.description: Tesla proposes returning study deposit value via transmission credits rather than forfeiting it.

bins.name: Colocation by withdrawal rights
bins.description: Tesla strongly endorses studying hybrid facilities on the basis of injection and withdrawal rights to incentivize colocation, and asks that behind-the-meter load and storage count toward the point of interconnection limit and toward offsetting generation loss.

bins.name: Behind-the-meter counts to POI
bins.description: Tesla supports allocating hybrid facility study and service by requested rights, and wants behind-the-meter load and battery storage counted against the contracted POI capacity.

bins.name: Expedited review for offsetting storage
bins.description: Tesla wants capacity additions behind the same POI that offset generation loss to serve load permitted through expedited review, and frames its Gigafactory case as evidence of the need for faster, more predictable interconnection.

bins.name: Minimum interconnection standards
bins.description: Tesla recommends standardized minimum interconnection standards for large loads covering ramp rate, load stability, and voltage ride-through.

bins.name: SPP and ERCOT as model
bins.description: Tesla points to performance expectations being developed in regions such as SPP and ERCOT as a possible foundation for national best practices.

bins.name: Technology-neutral load standards
bins.description: Tesla wants minimum interconnection standards applied at the POI through a technology-neutral lens, built on existing regional practice, and argues that once a load meets them no further disclosure of its changing makeup should be required.

bins.name: Clearer interconnection process
bins.description: Drawing on its Gigafactory Texas experience, Tesla urges greater clarity, efficiency, and predictability in the interconnection process as large load growth accelerates.

bins.name: DERs and VPPs as capacity
bins.description: Tesla urges the Commission to recognize distributed energy resources and virtual power plants as grid service tools that can support faster large-load interconnection and relieve transmission constraints and capacity needs.

bins.name: Fix Order 2222 barriers
bins.description: Tesla argues restrictive interpretations of Order No. 2222 still limit DER participation and asks FERC to address metering and telemetry barriers, device size thresholds, state-level barriers, and the lack of pathways for DERs receiving retail rate credits.
